title: GPT-5.6 about to DROP
summary: Wes Roth's YouTube video announces upcoming AI news and product rollouts. Coverage focuses on developments from OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, NVIDIA, and open source projects, framed around imminent release of GPT-5.6 and AGI readiness.
writeup: This video covers three converging AI developments: Anthropic's confidential IPO filing (which will force the first public look at real AI revenue, margins, and retention data), Claude Opus 4.8's state-of-the-art result on ARC-AGI 3, and rumored imminent release of GPT-5.6. The through-line is accountability: IPO disclosures will test whether the AI boom rests on solid economics, while new benchmarks like ARC-AGI 3 and Deep SWE are pushing past memorization to test fluid intelligence and genuine coding skill.
key_points: Anthropic's confidential IPO filing, at a valuation near $965 billion, will compel public disclosure of actual revenue growth, gross margins, inference costs, and customer concentration, turning the AI boom's financial claims into a verifiable balance sheet for the first time.

On ARC-AGI 3, Claude Opus 4.8 reached 1.5% accuracy, setting a new state-of-the-art; while still a very low absolute score, ARC Prize observers noted the model reasoned at a higher abstraction level than prior models, treating grid objects as mental models rather than raw pixels.

On the Deep SWE software engineering benchmark, Claude Opus 4.8 in extra-high and max effort modes did not outperform GPT-5.5 at any tested effort level; the ultra-code effort mode was notably absent from the benchmark results, leaving the competitive ceiling unclear.

The presenter built a custom LLM benchmark: a fully autonomous economic simulation (agents, businesses, wages, supply and demand, taxes, welfare) built entirely by Opus 4.8 in ultra-code mode, which also ran test iterations, wrote notes on what failed, and patched its own outputs.

The presenter's stated methodology for model evaluation is to build multiple independent benchmarks (targeting up to 10 distinct capabilities) rather than relying on third-party results or benchmarks whose answers may have leaked into training data.
